title: Transcript Analysis for Tax Resolution Practices
description: How resolution-focused tax professionals use IRS transcript analysis to build stronger cases, move faster, and deliver better outcomes for clients.
content:
Why Transcripts Are the Foundation of Resolution Work

Every IRS resolution case starts with transcripts. Collection Due Process hearings, Offers in Compromise, installment agreements, penalty abatements — none of these can be properly prepared without a complete transcript picture. The question isn't whether to pull transcripts; it's how fast you can analyze them.

Critical Codes for Resolution Practitioners

  • Code 520 — Collection suspended (bankruptcy or litigation)
  • Code 530 — Currently Not Collectible status
  • Code 582 — Federal tax lien filed
  • Code 971 + TC 971 AC 069 — Notice of intent to levy
  • Code 480/481/482 — Offer in Compromise indicators
  • Code 290 with zero amount — Audit closed with no change

Transcript Review Workflow for Resolution Cases

  1. Pull Account Transcript for all open tax years
  2. Pull Record of Account to cross-reference assessments
  3. Run all transcripts through parser to identify all codes
  4. Flag collection activity codes (582, 971, 520)
  5. Calculate CSED (Collection Statute Expiration Date) from assessment dates
  6. Identify penalty codes to assess abatement potential
  7. Build timeline of IRS actions for case strategy

CSED Calculation

The Collection Statute Expiration Date is 10 years from the date of assessment (code 150 date). Certain events — bankruptcy filings, OIC submissions, installment agreement defaults — toll the CSED. Accurate CSED calculation requires a complete transcript history and knowledge of tolling events.

Using Parser Reports in Client Communications

Resolution clients are often anxious and confused. A plain-English transcript report — explaining each code in simple terms — is one of the most effective client communication tools available. It demonstrates your expertise and builds trust at a critical moment.
